body.para: Ghidra is the NSA's open-source software reverse-engineering (SRE) suite. In EmulatR it is the tool used to read the SRM firmware: once the compressed image is decompressed to a flat binary, Ghidra disassembles and decompiles it to C so that firmware addresses, functions, and behaviors can be identified by name rather than by raw PC.

The input is the decompressed firmware produced by the host decompressor (for the DS10, decompressed_ds10_v7_3.bin). Analysis itself is done interactively in the Ghidra GUI -- import the binary, let auto-analysis finish, then save. The repetitive part is automated: tools/decompile_ds10.sh drives analyzeHeadless against the already-analyzed program (with -noanalysis) and runs the DecompileToDir.java post-script to dump C source plus an index file. Because headless needs the project lock, the GUI project must be closed and saved first.

The project layout is fixed in the script: the Ghidra project lives at EmulatRAppUniV4/ghidra, the program is decompressed_ds10_v7_3.bin, and decompiled output is written to tools/host_decompressor/out/decompiled_src. One pitfall is pinned in the script: Ghidra 11.3.x must run on JDK 21. An early headless run on JDK 26 failed with osgi.ee=UNKNOWN because Ghidra's script compiler could not resolve the Java execution environment and never compiled the post-script, so JAVA_HOME is set to a JDK 21 in the script.

This is how firmware addresses cited elsewhere in the documentation -- the decompression spin, the decompress() entry, the hw_ret R2 at PC 0xd954 -- were located and confirmed against real decompiled C.
